speaker: interviewee
text: [TEST PATTERN BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: So your husband, he-- [TEST PATTERN BEEP] We're in Los Angeles, interviewing Alice Hemar on July 18, 1994. Ready to talk? Oh, I'm sorry. I'm not used to [INAUDIBLE]. OK. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so that was a very strong experience, which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] led to, to, to, to already [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] certain decision. But we still were not quite ready. However, the speed of the events in the ghetto were tremendous. That was shortly afterwards. After this happened, Henry couldn't go back to the same Luftwaffe, because we didn't want to, to see the other people there. And he got a job carrying heavy sacks with building material. It was very, very strenuous job for him. That was the probably 100 pounds or whatever. He wasn't used to it. It was burning his back. And he, he couldn't tolerate this. So this, this evening, he came home sick. His back was burn. And you know, I look at his shirt. So that was covered with lice. I have never seen anything like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that, that was in addition to everything else, was really too much to, to tolerate. It was a minor thing. And still, that was like the, the last straw, last hitting, the terrible shirt full of lice of this man that was always immaculate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he decided to stay home the next day. He said, whatever will happen will happen. I went to the factory. I work for [NON-ENGLISH], sewing the uniforms for the Russian front. So they were one side green and the other side white, that were winter uniform for Siberia. And this factory employed about 300, 400 people. And we were doing pretty good job. It was a big production, and we felt safe, until this day. It was in the middle of the day. And that was especially strong kommando, many people. They came with Ukrainian collaborators with vicious dogs. They surround the-- surrounded the block. And everybody had to go run downstairs, down to the street. I tried to hide. And, and this SS man noticed me, so he hit me. I had to join the rest. And we were chased straight to the [GERMAN], to the cars. And this time, I was already inside. I was on my own, without anybody protecting me. I was inside, it was full, crowded with people screaming, crying. We knew that's already the final hours. That was nothing-- no appeal from it. People were squished, just like sardines in the box. It was cold. And, and, and people were screaming, crying. Some, some were getting crazy completely. Some were vomiting. Some were-- already a smell of human bodies. And I knew one thing. I wanted to live. And there was my cousin. His name was Paul Haber. He liked us, we liked him. And I told him I will try to escape, to, to, run, run away. He said-- definitely he encouraged me. He said he will help me. He had a pocketknife. He said he will cut the barbwire, which was covering the, the small opening, the small window in this cart. And he said he will do anything and encouraged me absolutely to jump out. We were not too far away from the station, maybe an hour in the train, not too far away. But the train, nevertheless, train was moving. And he managed to, to, to move this wires, to cut it down. And he said, jump, put your feet first. And he pushed me out. And here, you know, the train was moving, probably was on the curve. It was November 11, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1942. It was 3 o'clock, maybe 3:30 in the afternoon. I jumped on my feet. It was winter in Poland. The snow was laying on the ground. I didn't hurt myself. And the train moved away. I laid down flat on the-- there were-- further there were some bushes. I laid flat. I decided to wait till it will get dark. I don't know if I was thinking or that was instinct, I suppose, or whatever, destiny. And probably about an hour, hour and a half, when it got dark, I saw a light at one spot. And I was started to walk. Of course, I put my-- pull out my Star of David that we were wearing all the time. And I walked towards the light. And luckily, that was the street, that was the area, was opening to the ghetto. That was the gate to the ghetto. And evening, people that work outside on the, on the labor camp, day laborers, were coming back home to sleep in the ghetto. I approached the group. I told them what happened, that I want to go back to the ghetto, if they can help me. And of course, you see, they are being counted every morning and evening. So-- but they gave me priority, and somebody else tried to sneak in. Because that was one head more than left in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow, anyway, they protected me. They helped me. And here I came back to the ghetto, where my-- Henry, and Sophie, and my aunt lived there also, my aunt, Irene Haber. They knew already that I, I was on this transport. They didn't count on me anymore. And here I come back. So that was an unbelievable emotional scene. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- we were all getting out of our mind. And that was the final decision. That's it, not anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next morning, my aunt left the ghetto blindly, without any paper, just she went to the Aryan part of Warsaw. She ended up in a monastery-- in a cloister, monastery, with nuns. That's where she survived the war. And Henry started to contact people that we have known that belong to Polish underground army. And he told them that we want to escape, three of us. Sophie and us, we want to escape the ghetto if they can help us. They volunteer, we had a mutual enemy, the Germans. And so he suggests that, that we buy the birth certificate of dead people, and which are being storage with the church archives. They got that for us. Now, like I said, we sold my, my engagement ring at that time. And that was the money that we bought our new identification, new personalities, our freedom or hope of freedom. But that was enough money just to buy this, this papers. We try-- Henry tried to prepared a hiding place on the other side of Warsaw. And I was hiding under the bed, I think, for the couple weeks till we were ready, you know, to leave. I couldn't, I couldn't put my nose out completely. I was so frightened after this escape from, from the train. But anyway, he had enough energy, so he prepare a place for us to go there and also prepared the escape route. So he work at that time in a backs-- box factory. So he built up the whole wall of boxes where we were supposed to hide, Sophie and me. And he work during the day. And in the evening, we were three of us would split, split to liberty, split to, to other side. So the day came, we loaded whatever possible. Because whatever we-- our belongings. So that was the only thing that we could carry on us. It was November 26, '92. And that was strong, severe winter. We were not used to walking. And in the morning, that was this march from the ghetto to the, to the Warsaw. It was terribly slippery. We were loaded, not used to, to this weight, carrying the weight and also to, to walk. But the people were helping us. And somehow, I don't know how, how was it possibly. It was a deadly march, physically. We arrived to this factory. And Henry put us in the spot behind the wall, behind the boxes. And we were supposed to wait for the evening. We never knew if he will make it, if we will make it. It was freezing cold. And we were trying to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] talk to each other, encourage each other. Finally, it was 6 o'clock in the evening, dark. Here he came, you know, embrace us and said, here we are going to a new life. And he engaged-- that was a carriage with the horses. And he said, here are our new names, new identities. We tore up completely whatever we have-- our old papers, our old names, photographs. I don't have my father's picture. We tore up everything. And we are going-- we are going to a new, new life. And we got one good advice, which was good for the rest of the life. Don't ask any question and don't be surprised. Because that's, that's how you can-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, you can blame yourself for, for, for making a mistake. So pretend that everything is-- you are very familiar with, and just go with other people. So that was the end of our old identity, our old lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a beginning of a new chapter. It was clean, more comfortable, but lonely living among strangers. Some were friendly. Some were dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry got a job from a friend, Armenian friend, that owned shoe factory. He gave him a job, because we didn't have money anymore. We didn't have any money to support ourselves, to pay the rent and food. So he had to work. I was trying to stay as much  home as possible. Sophie went to work in this small grocery family shop and mangle they have, you know, where they press the linens. So she was working there. And we tried to have as little contact as possible. It was very lonely life. We knew we lost all families and friends. We left ghetto behind and all the suffering people. And in April '43, that was the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising. We saw the fires, the flames, the smell from, from our house. We couldn't show any emotion towards the, the neighbors. But Henry went to the wall, and he said Kaddish in the wall. That was as much as, as he could do. We were crying at night. And that was nothing, that was the end of the ghetto. And that's how we survive on these assumed names. There also was in '44, that was Warsaw Ghetto Uprise-- excuse me, not ghetto-- Warsaw Uprising. So the Poles fought-- tried to fight the Germans. And the Russian led them. They stop in by Vistula. And they led them for six weeks, that was the fight. And we were also in the middle of it. But we survived this, too, where was danger, other kind of dangers, not as Jews, but as Poles, as people being in the middle of, of the destruction of the city, of the capital. We survived this, too. And we were waiting on the-- in the small village by Warsaw for the end, till the end of the war. And actually, we were on the Russian front. In front of our little hut, our place, that was a battlefront. And the Russian were running away, in front of our window. They would drop their rucksacks, their, their identification papers, their weapon, in front of our house. So we were witnessing the actual end of the war. And the Russian came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They, they were, of course, little dangerous. Especially women had to be protected, hidden. But Henry went out to, to talk to them, to greet them. And he met a very nice Russian officer. He happened to be Jewish. And he was very excited that Jews survived. He brought-- he came to the house-- to the place where we lived. He brought us meat and, and some bread and hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we waited there. That was January. That was this part of the Europe was first liberated. We started-- we were the first one to be in the war, and first one to be liberated. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was January '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Pruszkow. That was by Warsaw. It was a small village, rather.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Pruszkow?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Warsaw Uprising, which went in October, September, October '44. And it ended-- the war ended January '45. And I forgot to mention that I had a little baby. My older son was born shortly before Warsaw Uprising. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell us a little bit about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: About my baby? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that whole thing feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: This felt-- we were living on the, on the assumed names, assumed papers. And by the time when I realized that I am pregnant, nothing could be done. So we, we took a risk, we took a chance, you know. And he survived also this Polish Warsaw Uprising, which was a miracle. Because he got sick from all the bombing and dust, and he still survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when the war ended, we tried to get to Katowice. That was a city where my-- where Henry is from. And we tried to get his father's apartment. His father was a prominent man in Jewish community before the war. And that was a very nice place, which we tried to get early, right after the war. So Henry went first. And I follow him with my sister, and my baby, and another friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to talk a little bit about the pregnancy.

speaker: interviewee
text: About my pregnancy-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't have a maternity dress. I felt pretty good. I was working in the house. I was going to, to visit my sister in the Aryan part of the city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was doing everything possible. And you know, when the day of delivery came, we-- that was a curfew. You couldn't go out on the street. But Henry stopped-- that was just the streetcar going back to the, to their station. But he said, this woman is delivering the baby. So he changed direction and took us to the hospital. And my son was delivered very quickly. He, he was born very quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- the only contact was with the Polish underground that arranged for the-- for our papers, and for the place where we lived, the apartment. Because that was a partisan that was out. And we were renting his apartment in, in, that was part of Warsaw, in-- that was behind Vistula, Praga. That was the-- on the other side of the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any contacts in hiding with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got pretty friendly with our neighbors that live in the same house. They didn't suspect that we were Jewish. They liked us. Like I said, we didn't ask any question. And we were not surprised at anything. That was the best advice in life. And so we accepted-- we were accepted by them. We were trying to avoid whoever we know-- knew from before the war. And we saw in the street, we would run away, like some people would do the same thing seeing us. Because you couldn't trust anybody at, at, at that time. That was absolutely no trust. But, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but of course, we were in contact with Sophie and Irene, who was my, my, my aunt. And that was-- oh, and of course, that was this Polish family that knew that we were Jewish. This woman was my grandfather's nurse from before the war. And they have known. They were coming to visit us as our-- they pretended that they were our family. It was wonderful people, very righteous people. They-- not only that they were coming, they didn't get anything from us at the time. That was completely humanitarian gesture. But they-- we were invited to their house and, and fed good food and treated like human beings. Of course, after the war, we-- over 50 years till now, we were helping them financially, sending food packages and money. And also, I, I mean, they would be among the righteous people in Yad Vashem in Jerusalem. Now, I am send-- I am in contact with the daughter that was born after the war, because this people died already. So they were righteous people. Their name was Dombrowski, Dombrowki family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're leaning forward now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Oh, oh, oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. We have time for my--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's still rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Tell me, how did it feel when you finally knew that you can come out and say you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: To this people there?

speaker: interviewer
text: In general.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in generally-- you know, we didn't stay too in Poland. Because we knew that that's not a place to start life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But why you were still in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why we were staying in Poland? We were looking-- the main-- the main thing, we were looking for the families. And we were looking for our Jewish friend. And we had Jewish friends only.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found some?

speaker: interviewee
text: We found some friends, yes. They were coming. My brother-in-law, he came from Russia. He was glad that, that my sister was there. That was a Jewish girl that survived the war. They got married in '40, '46. And we had other Jewish friend. We didn't-- somehow, we didn't make new friends. It was a time of confusion. We knew that we wanted to, to start a normal life, to have family, to have more children, to emigrate. So we went to Germany to a displaced people camps and tried to, to get possibilities of emigrating. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk a little bit about the reunions with these Jews after liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: The reunions were-- they were very, very emotional. Because we didn't expect to see anybody, to find anybody. My aunt survived in Siberia, my mother's sister. And she came back. She saw us, and she saw a little baby still. So that was unbelievable. That was the hope. That was a-- that was a new life. We met some friends. We were trying to-- whatever anybody needed, to, to help each other, if that was needed. They were not-- we lost most of the friends and of course, families.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they react to a new baby, to a new Jewish baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: How-- they were surprised. You know, people were surprised. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a strange thing that happened, because the women didn't have period all this years. Nobody was pregnant. Neither did I. I don't know. So anyway, you know, I didn't apologize. That was a boy, healthy boy. So it was a new life and new future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of the immediate family-- did you have any, found any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only my aunt from Siberia came with her husband. And Sophie survive, and Irene. So that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Henry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Henry, of course. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but Henry's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry's family, yes, that was his aunt and cousins, in, in concentrations camps. They survive concentration camps. So that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you found out what happened really to those people that were taken from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That people that--

speaker: interviewer
text: That were taken to camps, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: That were taken camps? We didn't know. You know, we didn't-- we did find out after the war what was the death toll. That was the tragedy. When I went to Auschwitz six years ago, so that was for me like being in the concentration camp, the smell, the, the, the ovens, the whole atmosphere there. So then I got an idea what the concentration camp was, to see the, the, the chambers of torture. So that, that was-- but at least, I think, in the concentration camps, people were-- they didn't have to pretend. They were together. And, and they didn't-- we had to pretend, which was very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you found out right after the war what happened to these people in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we couldn't grasp this. You know, we couldn't understand. We had people, strange people, coming from concentration camps. They were still skeletons. They would knock to the door, to find out, to look for each other, to tell the stories. You know, to tell the stories, what they went through. So that was terribly emotion-- emotional. That was-- we felt that maybe we didn't suffer that much. We thought that we suffered plenty. But this people went through much bigger hell than we did. But that was, again, this instinct. Even this people, they started to eat slowly, to look for other people, to look for, for, for-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] started to build the life, for men who were looking for, for girls, for, for Jewish girls. So it was a little happier atmosphere. They wanted to get married, to start family. There were a lot of marriages. We were the ones that were invited to every wedding, because we were first married. So we had the honor to give away the bride of a groom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No future in Poland, anti-Semitism. It was a terrible, terrible memories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was not the ground that we would like to raise the family. We thought of going to either to Israel or to the United States or perhaps Australia. And we went to Ger-- to displaced persons in Germany. And we decided on, on the United States. We came here. We started to work. That was a wonderful country, which offer us a lot of choices, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were in Germany before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Germany before. That was in Germany, that was a waiting period, where there were concentration of a lot of people from-- we made new friends, very close friends. That was a, I would say, physical healing like, in that we hate the Germans, they surrounded, their language. We had nothing to do with them. We lived within our communities. And we are--were trying to build up physically and to heal somehow, being with friends. And everybody was-- had some kind of destination. But they were new friendship, very close, which will still kept. And of course, here started new life. We wanted the children to-- we try not to inflict the, the sufferings in them. We wanted them to grow like their friends in school. They knew about the Holocaust. They knew how much we suffered. But they were not part of it. You know, the first time when they saw the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on television, that was first visual. And of course, later this showed them more, but still not to, to the, the extent--
